retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx -- Muscles -- Extrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The stylopharyngeus muscles stretch from the styloid process in the temporal bone to the thyroid cartilage. These muscles receive their nerve supply from the glossopharyngeal nerve, and their function is to elevate the larynx and the pharynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Stylopharyngeus Muscles -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Some of the fibers of the stylopharyngeus muscle become lost in the superior and middle constrictor muscles. Some fibers merge with the lateral glossoepiglottic fold while others join with the fibers of the palatopharyngeal muscle and insert into the posterior border of the thyroid cartilage/lamina.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Laryngeal Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Laryngeal innervation arises from the vagus nerve (cranial nerve X), which innervates the intrinsic laryngeal musculature. The extrinsic musculature innervation divides between the vagus nerve and the cervical rootlets.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Pharyngeal Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Although primarily known as the motor nerve of the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles, the accessory nerve (11th cranial nerve or cranial nerve XI) also contributes some fibers to the vagus nerve and indirectly influences pharyngeal muscular function. Sympathetic and parasympathetic autonomic nerves also contribute to the pharyngeal plexus. These nerves regulate the vascular and glandular elements of the pharynx but do not contribute to pharyngeal muscular function.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Stylopharyngeus Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: All the muscles of the pharynx receive innervation from the tenth cranial nerve (vagus nerve) except the stylopharyngeus, which is innervated by the ninth cranial nerve (glossopharyngeal nerve). From the nucleus ambiguous that lies in the floor of the fourth ventricle, motor  fibers travel in the glossopharyngeal nerve and innervate the muscle. After its exit from the cranial cavity, the glossopharyngeal nerve descends in the neck, running anterolaterally to the internal carotid artery. On reaching the inferior margin of the stylopharyngeus muscle, the nerve gives off several branches that provide motor innervations to this muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx -- Muscles -- Extrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The inferior pharyngeal constrictor muscles extend from the cricoid and thyroid cartilages to the pharyngeal raphe. These muscles are innervated by the vagus nerve via branches of the pharyngeal plexus and recurrent laryngeal nerve and narrow the pharynx diameter to contribute to swallowing.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Pharyngeal Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The sensory innervation of the pharyngeal mucosa is less complex but no less critical. Cranial nerve IX provides sensory fibers to the upper pharynx and contributes mucosal membrane sensation in this region. Cranial nerve X adds to the general sensory perception of the pharynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Stylopharyngeus Muscles -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: During the surgery for neurogenic tumor of the cranial nerves, the superior control of the internal carotid artery can be achieved by dissecting the area present between the stylopharyngeus muscle and the styloglossus muscle. [7] Along with the other longitudinal pharyngeal muscles (palatopharyngeus, and salpingopharyngeus), the stylopharyngeus muscle causes pharyngeal shortening and plays an important role in pharyngeal clearance. This avoids postdeglutitive overflow aspiration. [8] [9] The contraction of the stylopharyngeus muscle flattens the piriform recess. Thus, helping in the clearance of the piriform recess. Glossopharyngeal nerve anesthesia bilaterally produces a dysfunction of the stylopharyngeus muscle resulting in the collapse of the dorsal wall of the nasopharynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Stylopharyngeus Muscles -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The stylopharyngeus muscle has a topographical relationship with the piriform recess, which is the major route for pharyngeal swallowing and serves as the largest pocket providing post-deglutition retention. The lateral border of this recess (lateral glossoepiglottic fold or pharyngoepiglottic fold) is composed of the stylopharyngeus muscle and the palatopharyngeus muscle. The ninth cranial nerve curves around the posterolateral border of the stylopharyngeus muscle to pass between the superior and middle constrictor muscles and reach the tongue.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Skull -- Muscles -- Glossopharyngeal Nerve (CN IX)
retrieved_docs.content: The stylopharyngeus muscle, which raises and dilates the pharynx, is innervated by CN IX.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Pharyngeal Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Inner Longitudinal Pharyngeal Layer
retrieved_docs.content: Stylopharyngeus: This muscle is the only pharyngeal muscle innervated by cranial nerve IX. The stylopharyngeus originates from the styloid process of the temporal bone and inserts onto the thyroid cartilage, blending with the fibers of the palatopharyngeus muscle (see Image. Muscles of the Cheek and Pharynx). Contraction of the stylopharyngeus muscle elevates both the pharynx and larynx. These longitudinal muscles elevate the pharynx and larynx, aiding in swallowing.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Pharyngeal Muscles -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The pharynx is part of the digestive system posterior to the oronasal cavity and larynx. This muscular tube is divided into the oropharynx, nasopharynx, and laryngopharynx. Pharyngeal muscles are organized into an outer circular layer and an inner longitudinal sheet surrounding the lumen. The outer circular layer consists of the superior, middle, and inferior pharyngeal constrictor muscles (see Image . Pharyngeal Constrictors). The inner longitudinal layer comprises the palatopharyngeus, salpingopharyngeus, and stylopharyngeus muscles. The pharyngeal muscles receive dual innervation from the glossopharyngeal (ninth cranial nerve or cranial nerve IX) and vagus (10th cranial nerve or cranial nerve X) nerves, facilitating synchronous food propulsion from the oral cavity to the esophagus. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pharynx Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The pharyngeal plexus provides the sensory and motor innervation to the pharynx. This innervation forms via contributions from the vagus and glossopharyngeal nerves, as well as branches of the superior cervical ganglion. Specifically, the motor innervation of the STP muscle is mostly from the glossopharyngeal nerve, while the vagus nerve supplies the innervation to the other pharyngeal muscles. The pharyngeal muscles have a high nerve-muscle fiber ratio of 1:2 to 1:6 which allows for precisely coordinated pharyngeal functions. [2] The innervation of the cricopharyngeus muscle is from the pharyngeal plexus and the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Laryngeal Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The suprahyoid muscles, which include the mylohyoid, geniohyoid, stylohyoid, and digastric, are innervated by different nerves. The mylohyoid is innervated by the mylohyoid nerve, a branch of the inferior alveolar nerve, which diverges from the mandibular nerve (cranial nerve V3), a branch of the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V). The geniohyoid is innervated by fibers from the 1st cervical nerve (C1) that travel with the hypoglossal nerve (cranial nerve XII). The stylohyoid is innervated by the facial nerve (cranial nerve VII).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ear Eustachian Tube -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The TT and TVP are connected via a fascial link that receives innervation by the mandibular branch of the fifth cranial nerve (trigeminal). The salpingopharyngeal (SP) is one of the straight internal pharyngeal muscles that contribute to the process of swallowing mechanism; it receives innervation from a branch of the 10th cranial nerve (vagus). When the SP and the other 2 internal longitudinal muscles (stylopharyngeus and palatopharyngeus) contract, this results in shortening of the pharynx and laryngeal elevation. The SP is thinly spread over the cartilage segment of the ET. The muscle is small and thin so that it can easily cross the pharynx and at the same time have an anatomical relationship with the palatine tonsil. As of now, there is a scarcity of information on the exact physiological function of the SP, but most anatomists believe that it only plays a minor role in the opening of the ET.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Muscles -- Extrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The extrinsic muscles of the larynx are found in bilateral pairs and contribute to the gross movement of the larynx. The innervation of these muscles varies, involving the ansa cervicalis, trigeminal nerve, facial nerve, glossopharyngeal nerve, and hypoglossal nerve. The pharyngeal constrictors and palatopharyngeus receive innervation from the glossopharyngeal, vagus, and spinal accessory nerves through the pharyngeal plexus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 850
retrieved_docs.content: bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) when it involves nerve findings, and muscular atrophy/Bell’s palsy (وهن عضلي وبيل), which reflect the nerves’ integral role in motor and sensory function. Together, these relationships delineate how a diverse spectrum of nervous‑system disorders is organized by their pathogenic mechanisms and anatomical involvement, providing a framework essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: may be identified in relation to the thyroid gland, reflecting pituitary‑thyroid axis cross‑talk. Together, these interconnections illustrate how thyroid‑targeted pharmacotherapy, thyroid gland pathology, head‑and‑neck surgical interventions, and middle‑ear procedures are organized within a unified medical framework, emphasizing anatomical sites, therapeutic categories, and procedural relationships relevant to comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 264
retrieved_docs.content: pathologies, entrapment syndromes, headache and facial‑nerve disorders, musculoskeletal pathology and diagnostic imaging, thereby supporting comprehensive patient assessment, precise diagnosis and targeted clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2518
retrieved_docs.content: بضع طبلة الأذن is a specific surgical procedure that falls under the broader specialty of Head and Neck Surgery. As a targeted intervention, بضع طبلة الأذن is performed on the Head and neck structure, illustrating how procedures aimed at the tympanic membrane are classified within head and neck surgical practice and are directly applied to the relevant anatomical region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy, which may be performed as either a partial (lobectomy) or a complete (total) removal of thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 789
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are situated within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum, indicating that examinations of the larynx and trachea are considered part of the general set of respiratory‑tract diagnostic activities. Therapeutic interventions such as Injection of larynx and Injection of locally‑acting therapeutic substance into trachea are each classified as throat surgery, reflecting that these injections are surgical procedures performed on the upper airway. Although throat surgery is primarily a therapeutic classification, it is linked to the larger diagnostic group of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum, highlighting that certain interventions on the larynx and trachea can serve both diagnostic and therapeutic roles. Recognizing this overlap helps clinicians select appropriate procedures and coordinate care for patients requiring evaluation or treatment of laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2158
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx and injection of locally-acting therapeutic substance into trachea are both classified as throat surgery, representing surgical interventions that involve delivering agents directly into the laryngeal or tracheal structures of the airway.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1263
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly linked to the larynx, indicating that examinations and assessments targeting these structures focus on the laryngeal anatomy. The relationship identified as “indirect_procedure_site_of” connects the larynx to the broader category “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system,” meaning that the larynx can serve as an indirect site for non‑operative therapeutic interventions within the respiratory system. Within this category, the relationship “has child” designates the specific procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway) as a subordinate or child procedure. Consequently, the placement of an oral/pharyngeal airway is classified under “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” and may involve the larynx indirectly. Recognizing these interconnected relationships aids clinicians in coordinating diagnostic evaluations of the larynx with therapeutic airway management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1924
retrieved_docs.content: not considered forms of Excision. Excision is also linked to procedures involving the head and neck region. It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.
